repairBuyOrReplace.h2: 5 Series M550i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance on high-mileage N63 engines
Catch it early — engine-out job but cheaper than replacement
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Viable repair — common on early N63
Viable on a high-value M550i; often cheaper than engine replacement

buyingChecks.title: Buying This M550i? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2020 LCI G30 with the N63B44T3 (latest, most refined version)
* Post-2014 N63 with documented maintenance history (improved TU versions)
* Full service history with frequent oil changes (critical for N63 longevity)
* No excessive oil consumption or blue smoke (valve stem seal health)
avoidIf: * Pre-2014 F10 with no history — the early N63 is high-risk
* Cold-start rattle and no chain history — budget for timing chain work immediately
* Excessive oil consumption or blue smoke — valve stem seals likely failing
* No service history — the N63 is unforgiving of neglect
* Overheating history or unexplained coolant loss — cooling system issues may have caused damage

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The M550i is BMW's M Performance V8 5 Series — a 4.4-litre twin-turbo hot-vee V8 with up to 530 hp in LCI form. The N63 engine has a reputation for being one of BMW's most demanding engines. Early pre-2014 versions are particularly risky — timing chain stretch, oil consumption, and injector failure are common. Later TU2 and TU3 versions (G30) are significantly improved, with the N63B44T3 (2020+) shared with the M850i offering the best reliability of the family. However, all N63s require obsessive maintenance — frequent oil changes, careful monitoring of oil consumption, and prompt attention to any cooling system issues. With typical values ranging from £15,000 (F10) to £40,000+ (LCI G30), an engine replacement is a major financial commitment. A post-2020 LCI G30 with documented history is the safest choice — but this is not an engine for the faint-hearted.

cta.label: → Compare M550i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 5 Series M550i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling (timing chain warning) — see the map above
* Service History — Including which engine variant (N63B44O0/O1, N63B44O2 or N63B44T3) and oil-change frequency
* Oil Consumption — Ask about oil top-up frequency; excessive consumption may indicate valve stem seal wear
* Blue Smoke — Check on startup; any blue smoke indicates oil burning
* Coolant Level — Check reservoir when cold; any low or loss may indicate cooling system leaks
* Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any lag, whistling, or hesitation?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate injector or timing problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

items.question: How much does a BMW 5 Series M550i engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned M550i engine typically costs £6,000–£12,000 supply only, with the N63B44T3 variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 depending on the specialist. A blown V8 engine replacement can cost between £9,000 and £13,000. Dealer estimates often range £14,000–£18,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your 5 Series.

items.question: Is the BMW 5 Series M550i engine reliable?
items.answer: The N63 V8 is one of BMW's most demanding engines. Pre-2014 versions are particularly risky — timing chain stretch, excessive oil consumption, and injector failure are common. Later TU2 and TU3 versions (G30) are significantly improved, with the N63B44T3 (2020+) shared with the M850i offering the best reliability of the family. However, all N63s require obsessive maintenance — frequent oil changes and careful monitoring. Independent teardown experts uniformly warn consumers away from pre-2014 N63 models.

items.question: What are the common problems with the BMW 5 Series M550i?
items.answer: Excessive oil consumption from worn valve stem seals (can burn up to 1 quart per 1,000 miles). Timing chain stretch on early N63 engines (pre-2014). Coolant leaks from hoses, reservoirs, and radiator. Injector failure (common on early N63). The hot-vee design puts significant thermal stress on components. Later TU versions fixed several weak points.

items.question: Is it worth replacing an M550i engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the generation and vehicle condition. Against a well-kept LCI G30 worth £30,000–£40,000, a £8,000–£10,000 recon is 20–30% of the car's value — often worth it. Against a tired F10 worth £10,000–£15,000, the same recon may exceed our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. With repair costs often exceeding 30 hours of labour, many cars are written off rather than repaired.

items.question: How do I find my BMW 5 Series M550i engine code?
items.answer: F10 (2011–2016): N63B44O0 / N63B44O1. G30 pre-LCI (2017–2019): N63B44O2. G30 LCI (2020–2023): N63B44T3. Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation.

items.question: Can I repair the N63 timing chain, or does it need replacement?
items.answer: The timing chain and guides must be replaced if stretched or worn. A preventative replacement costs £3,000–£5,000 (engine-out job). A snapped chain almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £6,000–£9,500+ replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately.
